The Governance Gap in Companion Chatbots (Fathom AI)
Why AI companions need public health regulation, not tech oversight (Brookings)

verification.disputed_claims: Two soft spots. (1) The cross-source-claim 'commercial sentiment/adoption pressure will dominate over safety tuning' is inferential and unverified — I found no source establishing this causal dominance; it is the candidate's own flagged assumption and remains contested/speculative. (2) The warmth->jailbreak robustness link rests substantially on ONE recent preprint (Cheung & Yang, 2606.27709, ~4 days old, 4 models, not yet peer-reviewed/replicated); however its DIRECTION is corroborated by the broader well-established finding that fine-tuning (even benign) degrades safety and induces emergent misalignment (Betley et al. and multiple 2025-26 papers), so the mechanism is plausible but the specific 'warmth persona' magnitude is single-source. Note also a citation-accuracy issue: the risk's 'derived_from' ID 2606.27709 maps to the jailbreak paper, while the reliability/sycophancy finding it also cites is actually 2507.21919 — the risk conflates two distinct papers. No source directly DISPUTES that warmth tuning can weaken robustness.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence and de-claim novelty. The technical mechanism (warmth/agreeableness tuning weakening jailbreak robustness) is NOT unsurfaced — it is the explicit subject of a just-published preprint and a corollary of the well-established 'fine-tuning degrades safety / emergent misalignment' literature; brief it as 'corroborating an emerging, single-lab finding,' not as a discovery. The companion-chatbot regulatory-gap framing is likewise already in public discourse. The genuinely fresh contribution is only the SPECIFIC seam: warmth-tuning-for-adoption x scaled jailbreak surface x SB 53's catastrophic-incident threshold failing because harms present as helpfulness — surface that connection as the novel unit, with explicit caveats. Two load-bearing claims must NOT be laundered into confidence: (a) the 'commercial pressure beats safety tuning' step is unsupported inference, not a finding; and (b) the warmth->jailbreak magnitude is single-source and unreplicated. The SB 53 gap reading is a reasonable but untested statutory interpretation (the Act's 'critical safety incident' is built around catastrophic risk, loss of control, and weight exfiltration, which plausibly excludes diffuse 'helpfulness' harms) — present it as a hypothesis about a coverage gap, not an established gap. Net: moderate-to-low confidence; frame as 'a fresh synthesis over already-public components,' not a novel risk.

risks.risk: Verifier-guided RL is being exported into wet-lab biology and single-cell perturbation prediction with verifiers that check biological *consistency*, not biosafety — building a dual-use capability scaffold whose oversight layer is optimized for the wrong target.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight DECELERATING (-5.6%/qtr) while the verifier/RL machinery it underpins is being borrowed into applied science; this week 'PerturbCellRL: Verifier-Guided RL for Single-Cell Perturbation Prediction' (2606.27752) tagged RL + Scalable Oversight + Self-Improvement.
risks.derived_from: Paper 2606.27752 using a verifier to check generated cell responses for 'biological consistency' to reduce wet-lab screening; co-tagging of Scalable Oversight + Self-Improvement on a perturbation-prediction model; backdrop oversight deceleration vs capability acceleration.
risks.communities: Computational-biology / ML-for-science teams see verifier-guided RL as a way to make perturbation predictions cheaper and more biologically plausible. Biosecurity / dual-use governance specialists track DNA-synthesis screening and dangerous-capability evals. The seam: the scalable-oversight community's verifier idea is being repurposed as a domain plausibility check in biology, but a verifier tuned for 'is this a realistic cell response' is structurally orthogonal to 'is this guiding toward a hazardous intervention' — and the bio-ML authors aren't framing their verifier as a safety component at all.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: adding a verifier makes a generative bio model safer/more trustworthy. Inversion: a consistency-optimized verifier can *increase* dual-use risk by making implausible-but-hazardous predictions get filtered toward plausible ones — i.e., it sharpens the tool without adding any safety constraint, while the 'verifier-guided' label signals safety it does not provide.
risks.why_underdiscussed: This is a single applied paper in a science venue, invisible to AI-safety governance and to biosecurity policy (which focuses on synthesis screening and frontier-LLM bio uplift, not RL verifiers in perturbation models). The two communities don't share venues.
risks.mechanism: Scalable-oversight verifier methods diffuse into ML-for-biology → 'biological consistency' verifier reduces cost of perturbation screening → capability to predict/optimize cellular interventions improves → the verifier is mistaken (by reviewers, funders, or regulators) for a safety layer → no biosafety-targeted oversight is added because 'it already has a verifier' → cheaper, more accurate intervention prediction with a hollow oversight veneer.
risks.leading_indicator: Wet-lab or biotech adoption of verifier-guided perturbation models where the verifier's reward is purely consistency/plausibility-based; or grant/regulatory language treating an ML 'verifier' as satisfying a biosafety-review requirement.
risks.calibration: Genuinely low-probability/early — one paper, no evidence of deployment or hazardous use. The dual-use leap is a structural extrapolation, not demonstrated; I lower confidence accordingly. The novel and defensible part is the conflation-of-verifier-types seam, not a claim that this specific paper is dangerous.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed verifier-guided perturbation methods will be adopted operationally and read as safety mechanisms; the digest shows only the method, not any misuse, deployment, or regulatory treatment.

verification.prior_coverage: Every component of this risk is already in active public discussion: verifier/RL-guided single-cell foundation models and 'consistency' verifiers for perturbation prediction (bioRxiv 2025; cycle-consistency methods in Bioinformatics 2024), scalable-oversight verifier research (arXiv 2504.03731; NeurIPS 2024), the AIxBio dual-use/biosecurity-oversight-gap literature (PLOS Comp Biol; Council on Strategic Risks; NIST AI 800-1), and the 'verifier/metric is not safety' Goodhart critique. What appears genuinely fresh is the precise framing that a biological-CONSISTENCY verifier could be mistaken for a biosafety oversight layer ('it already has a verifier') — that specific conflation-of-verifier-types seam was not found stated explicitly, though adjacent ideas (screening giving an 'unwarranted sense of security', oversight-as-veneer) exist.

Predicting single-cell cellular responses to perturbations using cycle consistency learning (Bioinformatics)
A Benchmark for Scalable Oversight Mechanisms (arXiv 2504.03731)
Dual-use capabilities of concern of biological AI models (PLOS Computational Biology)
Assessing Dual-Use Issues at the AIxBio Convergence (Council on Strategic Risks)
NIST AI 800-1: Managing Misuse Risk for Dual-Use Foundation Models
Take Goodhart Seriously: Principled Limit on General-Purpose AI Optimization
Deep-learning-based gene perturbation effect prediction does not yet outperform simple linear baselines (Nature Methods)
PertEval-scFM: Benchmarking Single-Cell Foundation Models for Perturbation Effect Prediction
LLMs Gaming Verifiers: RLVR can Lead to Reward Hacking (arXiv 2604.15149)

verification.disputed_claims: The risk's central capability premise — that consistency-verifier-guided ML meaningfully improves the ability to PREDICT/OPTIMIZE cellular interventions — is contested. Peer-reviewed work directly disputes it: Nature Methods (Ahlmann-Eltze et al., 2025) and PertEval-scFM find single-cell foundation models frequently FAIL to outperform simple linear baselines on perturbation prediction, especially under distribution shift; an Evaluating-Perturbation-Models bioRxiv review echoes that evaluation itself is unreliable. Separately, RLVR/verifier reward-hacking work (arXiv 2604.15149; rubric-RL studies) disputes the implicit assumption that a verifier reliably constrains an RL system — verifiers can be gamed via what they fail to enforce, which cuts BOTH ways (weakens the 'capability improves' link and reinforces the 'hollow oversight' concern).
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Calibration must be lowered further than the candidate states. (1) Prior coverage: the dual-use AIxBio oversight-gap, verifier-guided RL in single-cell biology, and 'verifier/metric is not safety' (Goodhart) are all already prominent, well-covered topics — so this is partially_anticipated, not genuinely unsurfaced. The only fresh element is the narrow framing that a biological-CONSISTENCY verifier gets mistaken for a biosafety layer; that specific seam wasn't found stated outright, but it is a small step from existing 'screening creates unwarranted sense of security' arguments. (2) Disputed dependency: the mechanism leans on capability acceleration in intervention prediction, yet multiple peer-reviewed sources (Nature Methods 2025; PertEval-scFM) find these models often do NOT beat linear baselines — so the 'cheaper, more accurate intervention prediction' premise is contested and should not be asserted with confidence. Net: present this only as a low-confidence, structural 'watch-the-seam' hypothesis — flag the verifier-type-conflation framing as the one defensible novel contribution, while explicitly noting (a) the dual-use/oversight backdrop is already widely discussed and (b) the underlying capability gain is empirically disputed. Do not brief it as a demonstrated or near-term dual-use danger.

risks.risk: Black-box API unlearning ('CBD') is being positioned as the compliance answer to training-data transparency and copyright/PII laws — but 'controlled behavioral divergence' suppresses outputs without removing knowledge, freezing a verification-by-behavior standard that regulators and auditors cannot soundly check.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight DECELERATING (-5.6%/qtr) — the discipline that would build sound verification of removal is losing relative velocity; this week 'CBD: API-Only LLM Black-Box Unlearning through Controlled Behavioral Divergence' (2606.27683) advances behavior-level unlearning tagged Scalable Oversight.
risks.derived_from: Paper 2606.27683 framing API-only unlearning to scrub 'sensitive, copyrighted, harmful, or outdated' info via controlled behavioral divergence (suppressing behavior, not verifying internal removal); co-tagged Mechanistic Interpretability + Scalable Oversight; backdrop oversight deceleration.
risks.communities: Machine-unlearning / interpretability researchers know behavioral unlearning ≠ knowledge removal and that black-box methods can be recovered via relearning or adversarial prompting. Privacy/IP compliance lawyers and regulators (California AB 2013 training-data transparency, EU GDPR 'right to erasure', copyright plaintiffs) treat 'unlearning' as a deletion remedy. The seam: lawyers will accept behavioral non-reproduction as proof of erasure; the unlearning researchers know that proof is unsound — and they're not in the rulemaking rooms.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: unlearning is the technical fix that lets AI comply with deletion/erasure law. Inversion: API-only behavioral unlearning lets a model *appear* compliant while retaining the information, and if a behavior-based audit standard gets written into law or settlement terms, it freezes an unsound verification regime that adversaries (and the model itself under relearning) can defeat — worse than no standard because it confers false assurance.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Unlearning is discussed as a capability advance and as a privacy nicety; it's not yet connected to the specific 2026 legal pressure (AB 2013, GDPR erasure, active copyright suits) that will demand a removal *standard*, nor to the soundness gap that black-box methods create. The deceleration of oversight research means the verification side is not keeping pace.
risks.mechanism: Copyright/PII legal pressure demands provable removal → API-only black-box unlearning offered as the scalable remedy because it needs no weight access → courts/regulators adopt behavioral non-reproduction as the acceptance test (it's the only observable for closed models) → standard codified in settlements or guidance → retained-but-suppressed knowledge recoverable via relearning/jailbreak → false compliance assurance at scale, with the unsound test now load-bearing.
risks.leading_indicator: A regulator, court settlement, or audit framework that accepts output-level non-reproduction as evidence of data deletion; or vendors marketing API unlearning as GDPR/AB-2013 compliance without weight-level or relearning-resistance guarantees.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence. The technical claim that behavioral unlearning ≠ removal is well-supported in the literature (lowers risk of overclaim). The claim that a behavioral standard will be *codified* is inferential and contested — I lower confidence there; it depends on rulemaking paths not yet taken. The lean on the oversight-deceleration metric is an attention signal, not proof that verification methods are absent.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed legal/regulatory bodies will reach for unlearning as an erasure remedy and accept behavioral tests (not shown in digest); assumed CBD-style methods are recoverable like other black-box unlearning, which the abstract does not establish.

verification.prior_coverage: Both pillars of this risk are already in the public literature. The technical claim that black-box/behavioral unlearning suppresses rather than removes knowledge — and that suppressed knowledge is recoverable via relearning, fine-tuning, or prompt/jailbreak attacks — is established across many 2024-2026 papers (Hu et al. 'Jogging the Memory,' Deeb & Roger 'Do Unlearning Methods Remove Information from Weights,' 'Prompt Attacks Reveal Superficial Knowledge Removal,' SoK 2506.09227). The legal/regulatory seam — unlearning being offered as the compliance answer to copyright/privacy law and the danger of treating behavioral non-reproduction as the acceptance test — is directly addressed by the prominent Stanford Law / Cooper-Grimmelmann paper 'Machine Unlearning Doesn't Do What You Think' and by 'UnUnlearning.' The specific 'CBD / controlled behavioral divergence API-only' framing appears fresh, but the underlying seam is not.

sources.title: Machine Unlearning Doesn't Do What You Think: Lessons for Generative AI Policy, Research, and Practice (Stanford Law / Cooper, Grimmelmann et al.)
UnUnlearning: Unlearning is not sufficient for content regulation in advanced generative AI
Do Unlearning Methods Remove Information from Language Model Weights?
Jogging the Memory of Unlearned LLMs Through Targeted Relearning Attacks
Prompt Attacks Reveal Superficial Knowledge Removal in Unlearning Methods
SoK: Machine Unlearning for Large Language Models
Does Machine Unlearning Truly Remove Model Knowledge? A Framework for Auditing Unlearning in LLMs
Governing AI Forgetting: Auditing for Machine Unlearning Compliance
Towards LLM Unlearning Resilient to Relearning Attacks (Sharpness-Aware Minimization)

verification.disputed_claims: Two of the risk's load-bearing claims are complicated by the literature. (1) The risk's implicit premise that a behavioral/non-reproduction standard is INHERENTLY unsound is contested: the Cooper-Grimmelmann policy paper argues that whether structural removal vs. output suppression is required depends on the legal obligation, and that in some cases suppressing data in outputs MAY legitimately suffice for compliance — i.e., a behavioral standard is not automatically a failure. (2) The claim that 'auditors/regulators cannot soundly check' closed models is partly outdated: a growing body of auditing work (2505.23270, 'Governing AI Forgetting' 2602.14553, 'Auditing via Information Decomposition') and relearning-resilient methods (SAM-based 2502.05374, ROKA) is explicitly building behavioral and probe-based verification and more robust unlearning — so the verification gap is being actively narrowed, not simply absent. The recoverability/suppression-not-removal claim itself is NOT disputed; it is the strongly supported part.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence than the candidate states. The core technical mechanism (behavioral/black-box unlearning suppresses but does not remove; recoverable via relearning/jailbreak) is well-established and NOT novel — it is the consensus of a large 2024-2026 literature, so it cannot be briefed as a fresh insight. The specific legal/regulatory seam (unlearning marketed as the copyright/PII compliance remedy, with behavioral non-reproduction as the de facto acceptance test) is ALSO already surfaced prominently, most directly by the Stanford Law Cooper-Grimmelmann paper and 'UnUnlearning.' Two of the risk's claims must be down-weighted: (a) the assumption that a behavioral acceptance standard is intrinsically unsound is disputed by the same policy literature, which holds that output-suppression can be an appropriate compliance operationalization for some legal obligations; and (b) the claim that auditors 'cannot soundly check' closed models is overstated, since behavioral/probe-based unlearning audits and relearning-resilient methods are an active, fast-moving research area. The genuinely fresh element is narrow: the particular 'CBD / controlled behavioral divergence, API-only' packaging and the worry that a settlement or guidance could prematurely codify a brittle behavioral test before robust audits mature. Brief this as a partially-fresh framing of an already-prominent debate, not as a novel cross-domain discovery, and explicitly note the policy dispute over whether suppression-based standards are acceptable.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

**CURRENT-STATE BRIEF — Scalable Oversight (as of mid-2026)**

**Government pre-deployment evaluation of frontier models (the clearest real-world analogue of scalable oversight).** On May 5, 2026, NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) announced agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier-model evaluation program to five major labs alongside existing partners OpenAI and Anthropic. These labs will submit frontier models for government evaluation before public release. *Updates standing context:* this is now a five-lab program, not just the three named partnerships; the Anthropic and OpenAI partnerships were first signed in August 2024 and were renegotiated to align with CAISI's updated directives and the administration's AI Action Plan.

**California SB 53 (frontier transparency/oversight obligations).** Confirmed in effect; frontier developers must notify the California Office of Emergency Services of critical safety incidents within 15 days of discovery, or within 24 hours if an incident poses imminent danger. *Refines standing context's "report safety incidents."*

**EU AI Act high-risk timeline.** *Significant update to standing context, which had only the Nov 2025 proposal.* On May 7, 2026, the Council and Parliament reached provisional political agreement on the Digital Omnibus, postponing standalone (Annex III) high-risk obligations from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027, and product-embedded (Annex I) systems to 2 August 2028. Formal adoption and publication are expected before the 2 August 2026 deadline; 2 August 2026 remains an active compliance date until then.

**Federal preemption (Great American AI Act).** Still only a draft. The 269-page bipartisan draft by Reps. Obernolte and Trahan, unveiled June 4, 2026, includes a 3-year preemption of state AI-development laws. *Standing context's "not yet introduced or voted on" remains accurate.*
